<!-- Source: https://www.chebfun.org/examples/stats/LeastSquares.html -->

<div class="chebfun-import chebfun-example-import">
<div class='page-header'>
<h1>Least-squares data fitting and <code>polyfit</code></h1>
<h2>Nick Trefethen, October 2011 in <a href='../'>stats</a><span><a href='/examples/stats/LeastSquares.m'>download</a><span class='sep-sm'>&middot;</span><a href='//github.com/chebfun/examples/blob/master/stats/LeastSquares.m'>view on GitHub</a></span></h2>
</div>

<div id='content' class="col-sm-12 example" role="main">
<p>In MATLAB, a standard command for least-squares fitting by a polynomial to a set of discrete data points is <code>polyfit</code>.  The polynomial returned by <code>polyfit</code> is represented in MATLAB's usual manner by a vector of coefficients in the monomial basis.</p>
<p>In Chebfun, there is an overloaded <code>polyfit</code> command in the domain class that does the same thing, except that the polynomial is returned as a chebfun rather than a coefficient vector. Here is an example:</p>
<pre class="mcode-input">npts = 100;
x = linspace(-1,1,npts);
y = 1./(1+25*x.^2) + 1e-1*randn(1,npts);
f = polyfit(x,y,10,domain(-1,1));
plot(x,y,'xk','markersize',12)
hold on, plot(f,'r')
title('Discrete polynomial least-squares fit')</pre>

<p><img src="../../../images/stats/LeastSquares_01.png" class="figure chebfun-figure" alt=""></p>
<p>Chebfun also has a <code>polyfit</code> command in the chebfun class, and this is for continuous rather than discrete polynomial least-squares fitting.  For example, here is a least-squares fit to a jagged function:</p>
<pre class="mcode-input">f = chebfun('abs(x+.2)-.5*sign(x-.5) ','splitting','on');
r = polyfit(f,10);
hold off, plot(f,'k',r,'r')
title('Continuous polynomial least-squares fit')</pre>

<p><img src="../../../images/stats/LeastSquares_02.png" class="figure chebfun-figure" alt=""></p></div>
        </div>
    </div>
</div>
